heroOverview: Gnathostomiasis is a tissue-invasive parasitic infection caused by third-stage larvae of Gnathostoma spinigerum and related species, acquired by eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish, amphibians, reptiles, or poultry. Unlike purely intestinal helminths, Gnathostoma larvae migrate through subcutaneous tissue, muscle, viscera, and — in the most dangerous form — the central nervous system. The condition is endemic in South-East Asia and Latin America; Thailand and Japan have the highest documented case numbers globally, but travellers returning from endemic regions increasingly present in Europe and North America. The defining clinical sign is a migratory swelling — a moving, subcutaneous mass that shifts position over days — caused by the larva burrowing through the skin. CNS involvement, occurring in an estimated 1-3% of cases, is a medical emergency carrying significant morbidity and mortality.

keyStats.prevalence: Endemic in Thailand, Japan, Mexico, Ecuador, and neighbouring countries; exact global burden uncertain due to under-reporting; Thailand estimates several thousand cases annually
keyStats.demographics: All ages; adults who consume raw fish, frogs, or poultry in endemic settings; travellers returning from endemic regions
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases in adults 20-60; no strong sex predilection; rising in returning travellers
keyStats.globalCases: True global burden underestimated; reported cases have increased substantially with growth in international travel and raw fish cuisine globally

definition: Gnathostomiasis (ICD-10: B83.1) is a zoonotic parasitic infection of human soft tissues and organs caused by the third-stage advanced larva (L3+) of nematodes in the genus Gnathostoma, family Gnathostomatidae. The most clinically important species causing human disease is Gnathostoma spinigerum, though G. hispidum, G. doloresi, G. nipponicum, and G. binucleatum also infect humans. In their natural life cycle, Gnathostoma adults inhabit the stomach wall of definitive hosts — mainly cats and dogs, but also wild felines and canids. Eggs shed in faeces hatch in water; first-stage larvae are ingested by freshwater copepods (the first intermediate host), which are in turn eaten by freshwater fish, frogs, snakes, chickens, or other paratenic hosts where L3 larvae accumulate in muscle. Humans are accidental dead-end hosts who become infected by consuming raw or insufficiently cooked flesh of these intermediate hosts — raw fish dishes such as Thai ceviche (som tam with raw shrimp), Japanese sashimi from freshwater species, and raw frog or chicken preparations are the most common sources. After ingestion, L3 larvae penetrate the stomach wall and migrate through any body tissue, eliciting a striking eosinophilic inflammatory reaction wherever they travel. The larva cannot complete its life cycle in humans and does not mature into an adult or produce eggs; it may survive as a migrating larva for up to 10-12 years. The infection falls under infectious diseases and tropical medicine. The clinical spectrum ranges from transient cutaneous swellings to severe CNS disease depending on the larval migration pathway.

typesClassification.description: The most common form: the larva migrates through subcutaneous tissue, producing recurrent, migratory, non-pitting oedematous swellings that shift by several centimetres over days; associated with local pruritus, pain, and erythema. Swellings may persist for years.

Migration into the eye, lung, liver, kidney, or other organs, producing localised pain, haemoptysis, haematuria, or other organ-specific symptoms depending on the site invaded. Eosinophilia is usually pronounced.

Larval migration into the anterior chamber, vitreous, or subretinal space of the eye, causing iridocyclitis, uveitis, retinal detachment, or permanent vision loss; requires urgent ophthalmological management.

The most dangerous form: the larva enters the spinal cord or brain, producing eosinophilic myeloencephalitis with radicular pain, limb weakness or paralysis, altered consciousness, and possibly death. Case-fatality rate is estimated at 8-25% without prompt treatment.

primarySymptoms: Migratory subcutaneous swelling — the pathognomonic sign: a firm, erythematous, non-pitting oedematous lump that appears suddenly, lasts 1-2 weeks, then vanishes and reappears at a different body site days to weeks later as the larva continues migrating through soft tissue.

Local pruritus (itching), burning pain, and erythema overlying the migratory swelling; the skin itself is intact but uncomfortable; warmth and tenderness reflect the eosinophilic inflammatory reaction around the larva.

Initial gastric symptoms occurring 24-48 hours after ingesting infective larvae: epigastric pain, nausea, vomiting, and fever as the larvae penetrate the stomach wall and begin their early tissue migration phase.
Severe radicular pain — sharp, shooting, or stabbing pain radiating along a dermatome — which is an ominous early sign of neurological involvement as the larva approaches or enters the spinal cord.
Progressive limb weakness, paresis, or paralysis when the larva migrates through the spinal cord; can involve one or multiple limbs depending on the cord level affected.
Altered consciousness, confusion, headache, and neck stiffness when the larva reaches the meninges or brain, producing eosinophilic meningitis or meningoencephalitis.
Visual disturbance, floaters, pain in the eye, or sudden vision loss when the larva enters the globe or subretinal space; may be monocular and can progress to retinal detachment.
Haemoptysis (coughing blood), pleural chest pain, or breathlessness from pulmonary migration; chest X-ray may show fleeting infiltrates.
Haematuria, flank pain, or dysuria from renal or urinary tract migration of the larva.
Peripheral blood eosinophilia, typically marked (10-50% of white cells) and often the laboratory finding that first raises clinical suspicion of a parasitic infection in a non-endemic setting.
earlyWarningSigns: A sudden, unexplained subcutaneous swelling, particularly if it resolves spontaneously and then reappears at a different site over the following weeks.
New-onset severe radicular or shooting pain in a person who has recently eaten raw fish, frog, or poultry in South-East Asia or Latin America.
Marked peripheral eosinophilia (above 10%) on a blood count in a traveller returning from an endemic region.
Any neurological symptom — limb weakness, sensory change, bowel or bladder dysfunction — appearing within weeks of returning from an endemic country and eating raw aquatic foods.
emergencySigns: Sudden paralysis or rapidly progressive limb weakness — emergency hospital admission is required for suspected spinal gnathostomiasis.
Altered consciousness, severe headache, or neck stiffness suggesting eosinophilic meningitis or meningoencephalitis; needs emergency lumbar puncture and intensive-care-level management.
Acute vision loss or severe eye pain with floaters, which may indicate larval migration into the globe with risk of permanent blindness if not treated within hours.
Respiratory distress with haemoptysis and new pulmonary infiltrates in a febrile traveller with marked eosinophilia.

causes.cause: Eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish
causes.description: The most common route globally: L3 larvae concentrated in freshwater fish muscle survive refrigeration and light marination. Dishes such as Thai raw fish salad (koi pla), Japanese freshwater sashimi, ceviche-style preparations using inadequate lime treatment, and sushi using freshwater species are the principal sources. Larvae are killed by cooking to 63°C throughout or by commercial freezing.

causes.cause: Consuming raw or undercooked frogs, snakes, or reptiles
causes.description: L3 larvae accumulate in the flesh of frogs, water snakes, lizards, and other reptilian paratenic hosts. Traditional medicinal or cultural practices involving consumption of raw frog bile, raw snake bile, or whole frogs in parts of Asia and Africa are documented transmission routes.

causes.cause: Eating raw or undercooked poultry (especially chicken) that has ingested infected copepods or fish
causes.description: Chickens that peck at infected copepods in contaminated water, or that are fed raw fish scraps, can carry L3 larvae in their muscle. Raw or lightly cooked chicken preparations in endemic areas are an under-recognised transmission source, particularly in Japan where G. nipponicum is primarily transmitted this way.

A rare route: first-stage larvae from hatching eggs infect freshwater copepods. Swallowing copepod-containing unboiled water can transmit early larval stages, though this is a far less common route than eating infected paratenic hosts.

causes.cause: Dermal penetration of larvae from contaminated water or soil
causes.description: An exceptional and incompletely confirmed route: some reports suggest larvae can penetrate intact or abraded skin in heavily contaminated aquatic environments, producing localised cutaneous entry lesions; this remains less well documented than the oral route.

riskFactors.factor: Consuming raw or undercooked freshwater fish in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: The primary risk factor; populations in Thailand, Cambodia, Laos, Japan, Mexico, and Ecuador who regularly eat raw fish dishes have lifetime cumulative exposure; travellers who sample local raw fish cuisine once or twice are also at risk from a single exposure.

riskFactors.factor: Travel to or residence in South-East Asia or Latin America
riskFactors.description: Endemic areas include Thailand, Japan, Cambodia, Vietnam, Philippines, China, Mexico, Ecuador, Peru, and parts of Central America; returning travellers represent a growing proportion of cases diagnosed in Europe and North America.

riskFactors.factor: Traditional consumption of raw frog, snake, or poultry
riskFactors.description: Cultural or medicinal practices involving raw or minimally processed frog bile, snake blood, or raw poultry in endemic areas significantly elevate transmission risk beyond that of raw fish alone.

Immunosuppressed individuals — including those on corticosteroids, cytotoxic therapy, or with HIV — may harbour a higher larval burden, experience more severe tissue migration, and are at greater risk of CNS involvement.

Children may receive raw fish preparations as part of traditional weaning practices in some endemic communities; early heavy infection increases cumulative tissue migration and risk of serious sequelae.

riskFactors.factor: Drinking unboiled surface water from ponds or rivers in endemic areas
riskFactors.description: Open water sources in endemic areas may harbour infected copepods; boiling or filtering water reduces this minor but real transmission route.

affectedDemographics: Residents of rural and semi-urban communities in Thailand, Japan, Mexico, Ecuador, and neighbouring endemic countries
Travellers returning from South-East Asia or Latin America who consumed raw fish or frogs
Individuals who consumed traditional raw or minimally processed aquatic food preparations in endemic areas
Expatriates living in endemic countries for extended periods
Immunosuppressed patients with severe manifestations after relatively light exposure
Young children in endemic areas where raw fish is part of traditional diet
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosing gnathostomiasis requires combining a careful travel and dietary history, clinical examination, and laboratory investigation. The combination of migratory cutaneous swelling, peripheral eosinophilia, and a history of eating raw fish or other potential hosts in an endemic area is highly suggestive. Peripheral blood eosinophilia — often 10-50% of white cells — is present in most patients and is a critical screening finding that should prompt a parasitic workup. Serology is the main confirmatory test: enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and immunoblot (Western blot) detecting anti-Gnathostoma IgG antibodies against a 24-kDa larval antigen have sensitivities and specificities of approximately 90% in the hands of specialist laboratories. A positive serology result in the right clinical context is diagnostic. Definitive confirmation requires finding and identifying the larva — either emerging spontaneously through the skin, extracted surgically from a swelling, or obtained during ophthalmological or neurosurgical procedures — but this is achieved in fewer than 20% of cases. Cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) analysis in suspected CNS gnathostomiasis typically shows eosinophilic pleocytosis (eosinophils in the CSF) — a non-specific but strongly supportive finding along with haemorrhagic xanthochromia that reflects haemorrhagic tract formation by the larva. CT or MRI of the brain and spinal cord in neurological cases may show haemorrhagic lesions, oedema, or migratory tracts, though findings are often non-specific. The main differentials for migratory cutaneous swelling include cutaneous larva migrans (hookworm), sparganosis, Loa loa (in Africa), angioedema, and subcutaneous tumours. For CNS disease, the differential includes angiostrongyliasis (Angiostrongylus cantonensis), neurocysticercosis, viral and bacterial meningitis, and cerebral vasculitis.

diagnosticTests.test: Anti-Gnathostoma IgG ELISA or immunoblot (Western blot)
diagnosticTests.purpose: The main serological confirmatory test; detects IgG antibodies against Gnathostoma larval antigens with approximately 90% sensitivity and specificity at specialist laboratories.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn and sent to a specialist parasitology or reference laboratory; results take 1-2 weeks. The test is most reliable from 4-6 weeks after exposure when antibody levels peak.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count with differential (eosinophil count)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects peripheral eosinophilia (often 10-50% of white cells), the most consistent laboratory abnormality and the initial clue to a parasitic infection in travellers.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results available the same day. Serial counts every 4-6 weeks can track eosinophilia before definitive diagnosis is reached.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Required in suspected neurological gnathostomiasis; demonstrates eosinophilic pleocytosis and, in some cases, xanthochromia from subarachnoid haemorrhage caused by the larval migratory tract.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A lumbar puncture under local anaesthesia; the patient lies on their side and a needle is passed between lumbar vertebrae. Results, including CSF cell count and protein, are available within hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The neuroimaging method of choice; shows haemorrhagic lesions, oedema, and migratory tracts in CNS gnathostomiasis and guides surgical approach when the larva is suspected in a localised area.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient lies in an MRI scanner for 30-60 minutes; no radiation; gadolinium contrast is typically given to improve lesion visibility.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive diagnosis: identifying the Gnathostoma larva extracted from a subcutaneous swelling, ocular lesion, or at surgery; confirms species and excludes other migratory helminths.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The larva may emerge spontaneously through skin and be captured in a container of saline, or a subcutaneous swelling may be excised under local anaesthesia. Specimens are sent to a parasitologist for species identification.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when MRI is unavailable or to assess visceral or pulmonary involvement; may show haemorrhagic lesions, infiltrates, or migratory tracts, though sensitivity is lower than MRI for cord lesions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The patient lies in a CT scanner for a few minutes; an iodine contrast injection may be used. Results available within an hour.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of gnathostomiasis is guided by clinical form and severity. Albendazole at 400 mg twice daily for 21 days is the current standard first-line regimen and achieves clearance or sustained symptom remission in approximately 90% of cutaneous disease; it is thought to cause larval death or migration to the skin surface where it may emerge. Ivermectin 0.2 mg/kg as a single dose, repeated after 2 weeks in some protocols, is an equally effective alternative — a randomised trial from Thailand found a comparable 94-95% response rate for both agents in cutaneous gnathostomiasis. No large randomised trial has established the optimal regimen for CNS disease; most experts recommend albendazole for 21-28 days alongside supportive care and corticosteroids (prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day) to reduce CNS inflammation and oedema. Surgical extraction of the larva is curative when it can be located — either emerging through skin or identified on imaging — but attempts to blindly excise migratory subcutaneous swellings without imaging guidance often fail because the larva moves. Ocular gnathostomiasis requires urgent ophthalmological management, and the larva is often extracted by slit-lamp-guided vitreoretinal surgery or anterior chamber washout; anthelmintics are given concomitantly. Corticosteroids alone are contraindicated in gnathostomiasis (unlike bacterial meningitis) because they may facilitate larval migration without killing the parasite; they are only used as adjuncts alongside anthelmintic therapy in CNS disease to control the inflammatory response.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole
medicalTreatments.description: The standard treatment: 400 mg orally twice daily for 21 days (total 21-day course). Induces larval death or migration to the skin surface, where the larva may exit spontaneously or be extracted. Taken with a fatty meal to enhance absorption. Well tolerated; elevated liver enzymes occur in fewer than 5% of patients on 21-day courses and should be monitored.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves cure or sustained remission in approximately 90-94% of cutaneous gnathostomiasis cases; evidence for CNS disease is from case series showing response rates of approximately 80-90% with prolonged courses of 21-28 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Ivermectin
medicalTreatments.description: A single oral dose of 0.2 mg/kg, repeated at 2 weeks in some protocols. A randomised trial in Thailand found ivermectin equivalent to albendazole for cutaneous gnathostomiasis with a 94% response rate. May be preferred when albendazole is contraindicated (e.g., first trimester of pregnancy or significant hepatic impairment).
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Single- or double-dose response rate approximately 94% for cutaneous gnathostomiasis in the Thai randomised trial; limited data for CNS or visceral forms.

medicalTreatments.name: Adjunctive corticosteroids for CNS gnathostomiasis
medicalTreatments.description: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg/day, or equivalent intravenous corticosteroid in severe cases, administered alongside albendazole for CNS disease to reduce cerebral and spinal cord oedema and the inflammatory response to dying larvae or larval migration. Never given without anthelmintic cover.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical improvement in CNS symptoms typically seen within 2-5 days of combined anthelmintic and steroid therapy in case series; not evaluated in controlled trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Surgical larval extraction
medicalTreatments.description: When the larva is visible or can be precisely localised by imaging in a subcutaneous swelling, excision under local anaesthesia or guided extraction is immediately curative. Surgical extraction is also performed for ocular gnathostomiasis by an ophthalmologist using vitreoretinal technique or anterior chamber washout.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Curative when successful; success rate depends heavily on whether the larva is accurately localised before incision, as it migrates rapidly away from any inflammatory stimulus.

surgicalOptions.name: Subcutaneous larval excision
surgicalOptions.description: Excision of the migrating larva from a precisely localised subcutaneous swelling under local anaesthesia; imaging guidance (ultrasound or dermoscopy) is used to confirm larval position before incision. A simple, curative procedure when the larva is correctly located.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success rates exceed 90% when the larva is accurately localised before incision; lower when performed on the basis of clinical swelling alone without imaging confirmation, as the larva may have already migrated.

surgicalOptions.name: Vitreoretinal surgery or anterior chamber washout (ocular gnathostomiasis)
surgicalOptions.description: Slit-lamp- or fundoscope-guided extraction of the larva from the anterior or posterior segment of the eye; performed by a vitreoretinal surgeon under local or general anaesthesia to prevent permanent vision loss.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Visual outcome depends on the extent of larval damage before extraction; early surgery within 24-48 hours of ocular involvement is associated with substantially better visual outcomes than delayed intervention.

alternativeTreatments.name: Elimination of raw fish and aquatic food from diet
alternativeTreatments.description: For patients in endemic areas who are diagnosed and treated, complete avoidance of raw fish, frog, and reptile preparations eliminates reinfection risk; this is the most impactful single preventive measure after initial treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Safe food preparation: adequate cooking or commercial freezing
alternativeTreatments.description: Thorough cooking of fish, frog, and poultry to an internal temperature of 63°C throughout kills all Gnathostoma larvae. Commercial freezing at -20°C for 5 days has been shown to inactivate larvae and is applied to sashimi-grade freshwater fish in Japan.

whySeeSpecialist: All suspected cases of gnathostomiasis should be managed with infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist involvement because of the risk of CNS, ocular, or visceral migration. Any neurological symptom — limb weakness, radicular pain, altered consciousness — requires urgent hospital admission and specialist consultation. Ocular symptoms require same-day ophthalmological review. Travellers presenting to primary care with migratory subcutaneous swellings and eosinophilia after visiting endemic regions should be referred promptly rather than managed empirically.
doctorSelectionGuide: An infectious disease or tropical medicine specialist with experience in parasitic infections is the right first referral for suspected gnathostomiasis. Neurological involvement requires co-management with a neurologist or neurosurgeon; ocular disease requires a vitreoretinal ophthalmologist. For serological testing, a national reference laboratory or specialist parasitology laboratory is needed, as anti-Gnathostoma IgG testing is not available in routine hospital laboratories in most non-endemic countries.
preventionStrategies: Cook freshwater fish, frogs, snakes, and poultry to an internal temperature of at least 63°C throughout — do not rely on acid marinades or light cooking.
Ensure freshwater fish for raw consumption in endemic areas has been commercially frozen at -20°C for at least 5 days.
Avoid raw freshwater fish dishes (koi pla, freshwater sashimi), raw frog preparations, and raw poultry in South-East Asia and Latin America.
Drink only boiled, filtered, or bottled water in areas with unprotected open-water sources near livestock.
Travellers planning to visit endemic regions should be counselled about gnathostomiasis risk before departure, particularly if planning to eat street food.
Do not apply raw frog or snake bile to the skin or eyes — a documented traditional practice associated with ocular gnathostomiasis in some Asian communities.
lifestyleModifications: During anthelmintic treatment, attend regular follow-up to monitor for larval emergence at the skin surface and to assess treatment response.
If a subcutaneous swelling appears during treatment, do not attempt to squeeze or excise it yourself; notify your clinician, who may plan guided extraction.
Carry a medical summary card describing your diagnosis when travelling, as gnathostomiasis is unfamiliar to many clinicians in non-endemic countries.
After completing treatment, maintain dietary vigilance about raw fish and aquatic food in endemic areas — reinfection is possible with re-exposure.
Monitor for late neurological or ocular symptoms up to 12 years after potential exposure, as the larva can survive for over a decade.

recommended: Thoroughly cooked fish, frog, poultry, and other aquatic foods — internal temperature ≥63°C throughout
Commercial sashimi-grade fish that has been certified as frozen to -20°C for at least 5 days before raw service
Canned marine (not freshwater) fish, which is heat-sterilised and free of viable larvae
A nutritionally adequate diet to support immune function during treatment
avoid: Raw freshwater fish preparations (koi pla, freshwater ceviche, raw freshwater sashimi) in endemic regions
Raw or minimally cooked frog, snake, lizard, or turtle preparations
Raw chicken or poultry in endemic areas where traditional practices involve minimally processed preparations
Acid-marinated freshwater fish (lime, vinegar, citrus) — acid does not kill Gnathostoma larvae reliably

exerciseGuidelines: Physical activity does not need to be restricted in cutaneous gnathostomiasis, provided there are no signs of visceral or neurological involvement. During active CNS or spinal disease, complete rest and hospitalisation are required, with gradual physiotherapy-guided rehabilitation once the acute phase has resolved. Patients with residual neurological deficits (weakness, sensory loss) after spinal cord involvement should work with a physiotherapist and occupational therapist for structured rehabilitation.
dailyManagement: Take albendazole consistently with a fatty meal (e.g. full-fat yoghurt, peanut butter) to maximise absorption, at the same times each day for the full 21-day course.
Inspect the skin daily during treatment for signs of larval emergence — a visible, moving, pale worm at the skin surface — and contact your clinician immediately if this occurs.
Report any new neurological symptom (shooting pain, weakness, visual change) to your clinician immediately and go to emergency care if severe.
Have liver function tests checked at baseline and at 2 weeks if taking a 21-day course of albendazole.
Keep a symptom diary documenting the location, duration, and frequency of any migratory swellings; this helps the treating clinician assess treatment response.
prognosis: For cutaneous gnathostomiasis treated with albendazole or ivermectin, the prognosis is excellent: 90-94% of patients achieve sustained remission after a single 21-day course. The larva either dies and is absorbed or emerges through the skin, after which symptoms cease permanently. Some patients require a second treatment course, but cure is ultimately achieved in the great majority. CNS gnathostomiasis carries a much more guarded prognosis: case-fatality rates of 8-25% are reported in series from Thailand and Japan without modern intensive care, and permanent neurological deficits — paraplegia, sensory loss, incontinence — occur in 25-50% of survivors of severe spinal or cerebral disease. Early diagnosis and prompt combined anthelmintic and anti-inflammatory therapy improve outcomes significantly. Ocular gnathostomiasis, treated promptly, often preserves useful vision; delayed treatment is associated with a higher rate of permanent visual loss. There is no long-term residual infection state after successful treatment, as the larva cannot reproduce in humans.
recoveryTimeline: Cutaneous swellings typically begin to reduce in frequency within 2-3 weeks of starting albendazole or ivermectin. The majority of patients experience complete resolution of skin lesions within 4-6 weeks of completing the course. Peripheral eosinophilia normalises over 8-12 weeks. CNS disease recovery is variable: acute inflammatory symptoms may stabilise within 1-2 weeks of combined anthelmintic and corticosteroid therapy, but neurological deficits from cord or brain damage may persist for months to years and may not fully resolve. Ocular cases treated within 24-48 hours of onset generally recover vision in 4-8 weeks; late cases may have permanent visual impairment.
complications: Eosinophilic myeloencephalitis — the most severe complication, with case-fatality rates of 8-25% and permanent neurological deficits (paraplegia, incontinence, sensory loss) in 25-50% of survivors.
Permanent vision loss from ocular gnathostomiasis, including retinal detachment, vitreous haemorrhage, and optic atrophy when larval migration in the eye is not treated within hours to days.
Haemorrhagic stroke or subarachnoid haemorrhage from larval migration through cerebral vessels, producing sudden-onset neurological deficits.
Visceral haemorrhage: migration through the kidney, lung, or bowel wall can cause haematuria, haemoptysis, or gastrointestinal bleeding.
Prolonged migrating swellings for years in untreated or partially treated cutaneous disease, as the larva can survive 10-12 years in human tissue.
Scarring and permanent skin changes (hyperpigmentation or hypopigmentation) at sites of repeated larval migration in chronic untreated cases.

supportResources.name: WHO — Neglected tropical diseases: gnathostomiasis
supportResources.description: WHO resource on neglected tropical diseases including tissue-invasive nematode infections and their global burden.

supportResources.name: CDC Travel Health — Foodborne Parasites

sources.title: Katchanov J et al. Neurognathostomiasis, a rare imported infection: case report and review of the literature. Emerging Infectious Diseases, 2011

confusedWithConditions.name: Visceral larva migrans
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Visceral larva migrans (Toxocara) primarily causes hepatic and pulmonary organ infiltration with eosinophilia in children but does not produce the characteristic migratory subcutaneous swelling of gnathostomiasis; acquired from soil or dog faeces, not raw fish.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anisakiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anisakiasis is limited to the gastrointestinal tract and does not produce migratory cutaneous swelling or CNS involvement; it presents within hours of eating raw marine fish and is treated by endoscopy rather than prolonged anthelmintic courses.

confusedWithConditions.name: Enterobiasis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pinworm infection is confined to the intestine and causes perianal pruritus; it does not cause tissue migration, subcutaneous swellings, eosinophilia, or neurological disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Angiostrongyliasis (eosinophilic meningitis)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Angiostrongylus cantonensis causes eosinophilic meningitis from eating raw snails or contaminated vegetables, not raw fish; it does not produce migratory subcutaneous swellings, and the clinical course is typically more self-limited than CNS gnathostomiasis.

relatedConditions.name: Anisakiasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another tissue-invasive nematode infection acquired by eating raw fish; both require specialist management and involve larval migration in human tissues.

relatedConditions.name: Visceral larva migrans
relatedConditions.relevance: Another helminth condition caused by larval migration through human organs, producing eosinophilia and organ damage; classified in the same ICD-10 chapter.

relatedConditions.name: Angiostrongyliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: Another nematode infection causing eosinophilic meningitis, the key differential for neurological gnathostomiasis; both are managed by tropical/infectious disease specialists.

relatedConditions.name: Trichostrongyliasis
relatedConditions.relevance: A related intestinal nematode infection in the same ICD-10 category (B83); both are managed by infectious disease specialists with anthelmintic drugs.

faqs.question: What is gnathostomiasis?
faqs.answer: Gnathostomiasis is a parasitic infection caused by Gnathostoma larvae acquired from eating raw freshwater fish, frogs, or other aquatic animals. Unlike intestinal worms, the larva migrates through the skin, muscles, and sometimes the brain or eyes. It is endemic in South-East Asia and Latin America.

faqs.question: How do you get gnathostomiasis?
faqs.answer: You get gnathostomiasis by eating raw or undercooked freshwater fish, frogs, snakes, or poultry containing Gnathostoma larvae. Common sources include Thai raw fish salad, freshwater sashimi, and ceviche from endemic regions. The larvae survive acid marinades and refrigeration; only proper cooking or commercial freezing kills them.

faqs.question: What does gnathostomiasis look like on the skin?
faqs.answer: The hallmark is a migratory subcutaneous swelling: a firm, raised, itchy, reddened lump that appears suddenly, lasts 1-2 weeks, disappears, then reappears at a different skin site days to weeks later as the worm moves. It is often on the trunk, arms, or face.

faqs.question: Can gnathostomiasis affect the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes. In an estimated 1-3% of cases the larva migrates into the spinal cord or brain, causing severe radicular pain, paralysis, altered consciousness, or haemorrhagic stroke. This is called neurological gnathostomiasis or eosinophilic myeloencephalitis and is a medical emergency with a case-fatality rate of 8-25%.

faqs.question: How is gnathostomiasis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on dietary history, migratory skin swelling, and marked blood eosinophilia. Anti-Gnathostoma IgG serology (ELISA or Western blot) at a specialist laboratory confirms the diagnosis in most cases. Physical extraction and identification of the larva is definitive but achieved in fewer than 20% of cases.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for gnathostomiasis?
faqs.answer: Albendazole 400 mg twice daily for 21 days is the standard treatment, achieving remission in about 90-94% of skin disease. Ivermectin 0.2 mg/kg (repeated at 2 weeks) is an equally effective alternative. CNS disease requires the same anthelmintics plus corticosteroids to reduce spinal or brain inflammation.

faqs.question: Is gnathostomiasis curable?
faqs.answer: Yes, for cutaneous disease: 90-94% of patients are cured after a 21-day course of albendazole or two doses of ivermectin. CNS gnathostomiasis is more difficult, with recovery of function depending on the severity of spinal or brain damage before treatment; some patients have permanent neurological deficits.

faqs.question: How long does gnathostomiasis last without treatment?
faqs.answer: The larva can survive in human tissue for 10-12 years without treatment, causing recurring migratory swellings for that entire period. Without anthelmintic therapy, the infection does not resolve spontaneously. Treatment with albendazole or ivermectin induces larval death within weeks.

faqs.question: Is gnathostomiasis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Gnathostomiasis is not transmitted between people. You can only get it by eating raw or undercooked meat from an infected animal intermediate host. There is no risk of passing it on through contact, bodily fluids, or shared food with an infected person.

faqs.question: Can gnathostomiasis cause vision loss?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the larva migrates into the eye it can cause iridocyclitis, uveitis, retinal detachment, or vitreous haemorrhage leading to permanent vision loss. Ocular gnathostomiasis requires urgent same-day ophthalmological evaluation and is treated by surgical larval extraction combined with anthelmintic therapy.

faqs.question: What causes the moving lump in gnathostomiasis?
faqs.answer: The migratory swelling is caused by the Gnathostoma larva physically burrowing through subcutaneous tissue, creating an intense localised eosinophilic inflammatory reaction as it moves. The swelling tracks the larva's path — typically advancing a few centimetres per day — and subsides at one site as the larva moves to another.

faqs.question: Which countries have gnathostomiasis?
faqs.answer: Gnathostomiasis is endemic in Thailand, Japan, Cambodia, Vietnam, Philippines, China, Indonesia, Mexico, Ecuador, Peru, and parts of Central America. Thailand has the highest reported case burden globally. Cases in Europe and North America are almost exclusively in returned travellers from these regions.

faqs.question: Does cooking fish kill Gnathostoma larvae?
faqs.answer: Yes. Cooking fish to an internal temperature of 63°C throughout reliably kills all Gnathostoma larvae. Commercial freezing at -20°C for 5 days also inactivates them. Acid marinades such as lime or vinegar, salt curing, and refrigeration do not kill the larvae and are not safe alternatives.

faqs.question: What blood test shows gnathostomiasis?
faqs.answer: Peripheral blood eosinophilia — often 10-50% of white blood cells — is the most consistent laboratory finding and should trigger suspicion of gnathostomiasis in a traveller with migratory skin swellings. Specific anti-Gnathostoma IgG antibody testing at a specialist laboratory confirms the diagnosis.

faqs.question: Why is radicular pain a warning sign?
faqs.answer: Sudden severe shooting or stabbing pain radiating along a specific body segment (a dermatome) signals that the Gnathostoma larva may be near or entering the spinal cord. This is the most important early warning of neurological involvement and should prompt urgent hospital evaluation, as paralysis can develop within hours.

faqs.question: Can a migratory swelling from gnathostomiasis be removed surgically?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the larva can be precisely located by ultrasound or dermoscopy immediately before incision. Guided surgical excision is curative when successful. Blind excision without imaging confirmation fails frequently because the larva moves away from the inflammatory stimulus of incision before the surgeon can reach it.

faqs.question: Is gnathostomiasis a risk for travellers?
faqs.answer: Yes. Travellers to Thailand, Japan, Mexico, Ecuador, and neighbouring countries who eat raw freshwater fish, frogs, or traditional raw meat preparations are at risk. A single exposure to an infective meal is sufficient for infection. Travellers should be counselled about food safety before visiting endemic areas.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cutaneous and neurological gnathostomiasis?
faqs.answer: Cutaneous gnathostomiasis affects the skin and subcutaneous tissues, producing migratory swellings; it is treatable and rarely life-threatening. Neurological gnathostomiasis occurs when the larva enters the spinal cord or brain, causing paralysis, haemorrhage, or death; it is a medical emergency with a case-fatality rate of 8-25%.

faqs.question: Do I need to avoid fish forever after gnathostomiasis?
faqs.answer: After successful treatment, fully cooked fish is safe to eat. You should continue to avoid raw or undercooked freshwater fish, frogs, and aquatic animals in endemic areas, as reinfection from re-exposure is possible. Marine fish properly frozen before raw service (sashimi-grade ocean species) carries no gnathostomiasis risk.

metaDescription: Gnathostomiasis is a worm from raw fish that migrates under skin and rarely into the brain. Learn diagnosis, albendazole treatment, and fish safety tips.
